narration: What if your dog has been talking to you this whole time… and you just didn’t know the language?

under_narration.caption: a phone with a dog on the screen
a black background with the words can dogs talk?

narration: Because the truth is, dogs communicate constantly—through looks, movement, timing… and the tiniest signals we miss.

under_narration.caption: a white dog laying on top of a bed
a woman sitting on the floor playing with her dog

segments.transcript: [0.11s-1.21s] [SPEAKER_13] All animals communicate. | [1.86s-7.32s] [SPEAKER_13] All animals communicate and can understand communication, but not all communication is language.
[0.00s-3.36s] [SPEAKER_02] dogs are also paying attention to the way our body moves.
[0.00s-2.39s] [SPEAKER_15] for by combining other signals.

narration: But then something wild happened: people started giving dogs a new tool—buttons with recorded words—and suddenly it looked like conversation.

under_narration.caption: a dog with a toy in it ' s mouth
a graph showing the number of people who have been diagnosed

segments.transcript: [0.00s-2.41s] [SPEAKER_14] that seem to give dogs the ability to talk.
[0.00s-0.17s] [SPEAKER_14] buttons. | [1.87s-2.86s] [SPEAKER_10] Okay,
[0.11s-0.43s] [SPEAKER_06] Break. | [1.51s-1.89s] [SPEAKER_06] Button. | [2.98s-6.70s] [SPEAKER_06] Treat.

narration: Here’s the hook inside the hook: if a dog presses “treat,” are they using a word… or just pushing a vending machine?

[0.00s-0.64s] [SPEAKER_14] words mean. | [1.68s-6.37s] [SPEAKER_14] Perhaps they just enjoy the reward they get, whether that's a treat or just some attention.

narration: Scientists have a name for that: responding correctly doesn’t automatically mean understanding. A “sit” can be a trick… not a thought.

segments.transcript: [0.00s-5.71s] [SPEAKER_13] sit and to know that if they sit they will get a reward is a very, very powerful mechanism. | [7.47s-13.61s] [SPEAKER_13] But just because an animal responds correctly to a particular command that is given in language,
[0.00s-3.96s] [SPEAKER_14] incredibly hard to know how much dogs actually understand the words they're

narration: So the mystery becomes: can dogs use buttons in a way that’s intentional—choosing words, not just chasing rewards?

a graph showing the number of people who have been diagnosed

[0.00s-0.51s] [SPEAKER_14] different things? | [1.61s-2.61s] [SPEAKER_14] Could they choose which
[0.59s-3.01s] [SPEAKER_14] So could dogs actually use buttons in a way

narration: To answer that, researchers went big—way bigger than a single viral dog video.

under_narration.caption: a black background with the words can dogs talk?
a graph showing the number of people who have been diagnosed

segments.transcript: [0.00s-5.94s] [SPEAKER_14] data on the word buttons being used by an astonishing 10,000 dogs in almost 50 countries. | [7.28s-10.99s] [SPEAKER_14] It's the largest animal communication study in history.
[1.80s-8.45s] [SPEAKER_14] Federico's analysis of over 10 million button presses by thousands of dogs over almost two years
[0.00s-3.15s] [SPEAKER_14] automatically record which words are pressed and when. | [4.67s-6.75s] [SPEAKER_15] Every time a button is pressed,

narration: And the data hints at patterns: many dogs don’t press everything. They show preferences—certain words, over and over—usually the ones that matter to them.

under_narration.caption: a white dog laying on top of a bed
a woman sitting on the floor playing with her dog

segments.transcript: [0.93s-5.29s] [SPEAKER_14] So across the data set, dogs definitely show preference for certain words. | [7.07s-8.78s] [SPEAKER_14] Seemingly things they want,

[0.79s-5.92s] [SPEAKER_14] Of all the dogs taking part in Federico's study, most use just a handful of buttons. | [8.96s-14.11s] [SPEAKER_14] But there are at least 30 dogs who routinely use at least 50 different buttons.

narration: But preference still isn’t proof. The hardest question is intention: are they choosing words to share information… or just spamming buttons until something happens?

segments.transcript: [0.00s-3.35s] [SPEAKER_14] show whether dog button use is random or intentional. | [11.78s-14.32s] [SPEAKER_15] In principle, the dog could just be spamming the
[0.00s-3.23s] [SPEAKER_14] thousands of dogs taking part know what they're doing with buttons? | [3.99s-5.95s] [SPEAKER_14] Or is most of it just random?

narration: So let’s watch what “not random” might look like: a back-and-forth where the human offers options, and the dog pushes the moment forward.

under_narration.caption: a white dog standing on top of a wooden floor

segments.transcript: [10.88s-12.96s] [SPEAKER_14] Stella appears to be talking. | [13.33s-14.19s] [SPEAKER_10] You want to go to the park? | [15.59s-18.21s] [SPEAKER_10] It's a little late to go to the park. | [18.23s-23.59s] [SPEAKER_14] This video has been sped up a little, but nothing has been edited in or out. | [24.66s-26.16s] [SPEAKER_10] Do you want to go for a walk instead? | [38.56s-40.68s] [SPEAKER_10] Okay, we'll go for a walk. | [42.50s-43.36s] [SPEAKER_10] Let's get your leash on. | [46.87s-47.05s] [SPEAKER_14] Leash on. | [47.07s-49.28s] [SPEAKER_14] Stella has been taught to use word buttons.

narration: Now the bar gets even higher: can a dog use buttons for more than requests—like time, planning, or emotion?

segments.transcript: [0.00s-5.64s] [SPEAKER_14] a huge step would be if dogs were using words to do more than just ask for things.

[0.00s-4.91s] [SPEAKER_14] three-and-a-half-year-old Labrador, also seem to be using buttons to do more than just make requests. | [5.81s-6.71s] [SPEAKER_09] Outside pool. | [6.74s-7.60s] [SPEAKER_09] Outside pool. | [8.14s-10.74s] [SPEAKER_09] Outside pool, maybe tomorrow. | [15.95s-17.11s] [SPEAKER_09] Outside pool later. | [18.96s-19.38s] [SPEAKER_09] Later. | [23.47s-24.99s] [SPEAKER_09] Oh my goodness, copper. | [26.26s-29.00s] [SPEAKER_14] It looks like copper was using her buttons to express
[0.00s-1.13s] [SPEAKER_14] express emotion. | [1.68s-4.01s] [SPEAKER_14] But of course, we don't know for sure.

narration: And that “we don’t know for sure” is the point. Real language isn’t just making sounds—it’s exchanging information with a partner who’s also in the conversation.

under_narration.caption: a woman pointing at a wall with words on it

segments.transcript: [0.00s-21.32s] [SPEAKER_13] think really communication becomes language when you cross that boundary from a signal that manipulates other individuals to a system by which information is exchanged between individuals who understand that their conversation partner is also participating in that conversation.

narration: So here’s the payoff: whether or not dogs have “language,” they’re already incredible communicators—and the buttons may be less about turning dogs into humans… and more about meeting them halfway.

segments.transcript: [0.00s-5.69s] [SPEAKER_13] dogs to be excellent understanders of humans and humans to be excellent understanders of dogs as well.
[1.74s-7.17s] [SPEAKER_14] But with this extraordinary ability and desire of dogs to communicate with us,
[4.36s-14.44s] [SPEAKER_14] With a new tool in their communication kit and access to our words, perhaps the close bond between humans and dogs could become even closer.
